97265A, the Glen Allen Jagbots has been around for 5 years. As a team 
with 23 members, we are dedicated to continuous improvement, 
communication, and hard work. 
This engineering notebook documents the complete design, build, and 
programming journey of our VEX V5 robot from September through 
December 2025. It reﬂects our engineering process in its entirety, 
including brainstorming, failed attempts, redesigns, testing data, and 
ﬁnal solutions. Each entry demonstrates collaboration between Build, 
Code, and Strategy subteams, and decisions grounded in teamwork.

This year, we are committed to becoming

Identify
Push Back: Identify the Game Problem
Name: Darren and Avanti Date: 9/16
The VEX Robotics Competition game for 2025-2026 is Push 
Back. High stakes is played with 2 alliances each trying to 
score more than the other. The main scoring elements are 
the blue and red balls. The goals are very high so we need 
some height on the robot, there is also a low goal so we 
need to have a versatile robot. Since there are different 
heights to the goals, we will need to make sure the robot 
can output at different heights easily. Alliances could be 
hard for us because we  need a good teammate that 
specializes in a different aspect of the game to beneﬁt ours. 
Page: 6
Field Elements:
● Blocks: The main 
scoring objects
● Goals: Two 
types—Long Goals and 
Center Goals
● Loaders: Four stations 
that start with six 
blocks each

A hollow object with a height and width 
of 3.23” and a diagonal length of 3.84”.

Drivetrain
Selecting Designs
REASONS FOR SELECTING THE FOUR MOTOR 
DRIVEBASE
● Using 6 motors for the drive base alone, as 
we originally planned, leaves us with only 2 
motors for everything else. This causes 
problems so we switched to using only 4 
motors.
● 4 motors gives us enough power from the 
base to have a larger robot without weight 
issues.

Test of Speed
This test measures how fast the drivetrain goes

TEST OF SPEED (6 Motors)- Seconds
The passing score for this test was an average of 2.5 seconds. The 
drivetrain with 6 motors powering it passed the test quite easily, 
having more than half a second to spare overall. Test Passed.

Test Solution
Both versions of the drivetrain PASSED THE TEST. The surprising 
results show that the 6 motor drivetrain was slower than 4 motor 
speed. This could be due to chance though unlikely due to the 
number of trials we conducted. However, this discovery does not 
change our decision to use 4 motors to power the robot.

Intake
Selecting Designs
WHY WE CHOSE THIS SOLUTION
We chose our S-shaped intake because it has a 
very simple design to implement and more 
optimal ways to score. It was so much easier 
to use this design and put a gap at an end to 
shoot it outward instead of designing 
something more convoluted like our prior 
ideas. This design is also capable of scoring 
on all three levels, unlike other designs.

Team Meeting Notes
Team Meeting - 9/02
Drivebase
● 4 Motor Drive
○ Pros: 
■ Saves motors overall
○ Cons:
■ Requires more torque with more weight=less speed
● 6 Motor Drive
○ Pros: 
■ Faster on green motors (even with weight)
■ Can hold more weight on green
○ Cons:
■ More motors
■ Gear drive difficult to build together
■ Chain drive difficult to work on
● Selected Base: Six Motor Gear Drive
○ Because of what we saw from prior competitions as well 
as in-meeting testing we have determined that a 6 
motordrive is preferable because the increased speed 
and weight capacity outweighs the increased usage of 
motors. We also decided on a gear drive because they 
have been much more reliable. Page: 16

AGENDAS
Team Meeting Notes
Team Meeting - 9/03
Using Python
● We finally started a new code project named Imperium 
(Control in Latin) because this will be our base code we will 
use for our robots

○ Drivetrain code

Page: 34

Name: Maxwell Date: 9/03/2025
Team Meeting Notes
Team Meeting - 9/03
Drivetrain Code
● We wanted to be able to control each side of the robots 
motors in drive mode so we couldn't use the built in 
drivetrain in the code and we did not have an inertial 
sensor so we had 2 options left:
○ Time-based drivetrain, The time for the motors to spin 
is precalculated and it spins for that exact time
■ Pros are that its very simple
■ Cons are that motor efficiency can change over 
time, which a precalculation cannot account for
○ Rotation based drivetrain, how much the motor spins 
is determined by how much it has left to spin
■ Pros are that it can be very accurate if you stop the 
robot and let it go it will still go to its desired goal
● Can make robot slow down as it approaches 
the goal to prevent jerking of robot
○ Cons are that its very complex and easy to make 
mistakes in code
● Complexity was not a problem for usand so we started to 
work on the rotation based drivetrain.
Page: 35

Maxwell M 36
Rotation Based Drivetrain
This system will get how much the robot needs to move by using the math of the robots dimensions
● First we got how much the wheels must move with: to_move = ((math.fabs(D) / 
wheel_circumference) * 360) / 6
○ D is, in inches, how much we want to move
Then, we made a while loop that loops until to move <= 0, because to_move says how much 
rotations left the wheels have left to rotate
● we calculated the motor efficiency, velocity, and time it took to get from each frame with the 
equation: delta = (((velocity * (efficiency / 100)) / 60) * (elasped_time)) * 360 
○ Velocity = RPM
○ Was done for each side of the robot separately then we got the average delta
● We then used this to get how much the robot moved with: to_move = to_move + (0 - 
(math.fabs(Ldelta) + math.fabs(Rdelta)) / 8)
○ L and R delta are for each side of the robot respectively
○ 4 motors * 2 = 8, which is the divisor
● We also made the robot slow down as it approached the target velocity with: velset = 
(abs_tomove * (max_speed - floor_speed) + floor_speed)
○ abs_tomove = abs(to_move / starting_to_move)
■ Starting_to_move is the unchanged starting to_move
● All of this was put in a while to_move > 0 so once the to_move was 0 or less, the robot would 
set all of the motors velocities to zero, completing the movement
We also wanted the robot to turn with a system similar to the movement system
● We use the calculations:
○ turn_circumference = 2.0 * math.pi * (wheelbase / 2.0)
○ circumference = 2.0 * math.pi * wheel_radius
○ to_move = ((((math.fabs(d) / 360.0) * (turn_circumference / 2.0)) / circumference) * 
360.0) / 2.0
● In replacement of the original and the velocities of each side are either positive or negative to 
make the robot turn instead of just move forward

Name: Maxwell Date: 9/03/2025
Team Meeting Notes
Team Meeting - 9/03
Drive code adjustments
● Also made some adjustments to the standard drive code 
for easier driver movement
Page: 37
Made it so that the input 
smooths from the 
purple line to either the 
red or green line 
depending on the the v 
variable in f(x,v), with x 
being the joystick input 
from 0 to 100
This is because if the driver wants to do precise movements 
with the joystick, they have to slightly tap the stick, which 
is difficult to do in high-stress situations in competitions, so 
now just by moving the stick halfway, the robot can move 
slowly and of course jabbing the stick forward will still 
provide the maximum speed.

Name: Maxwell Date: 9/17/2025
Team Meeting Notes
Team Meeting - 9/17
Added new drive code mode, dual stick drive
● Currently we had 2 drive modes (they are just lying in the 
code)
○ Tank drive (standard tank drive setup, joystick drive 
controlling each side of the robot, give the most 
control)
○ Single stick drive (one stick controls both moving 
forward (up/down) and turning (left/right), gives least 
amount of control yet leaves a full stick for other 
controls of the robot)
● And now, we added dual stick drive!
○ It's the single stick drive but split into 2 separate sticks 
the left stick controls moving forward (up/down) and the 
right stick controls turning (left right)
● Although it uses 2 sticks instead of one, it's much easier to 
not have mistakes unlike the single stick drive
Page: 38

Name: Maxwell Date: 10/22/2025
Team Meeting Notes
Team Meeting - 10/22
Found robot torque problem
● On 10/21 we came across a problem, the drivetrain was 
not turning efficiently and on point every time so we turned 
down the torque to reduce strain on the drivetrain but it still 
didn't truly fix the problem.
● The speed of the robot can be visualised with this graph, 
along with our solution
● when moving a certain distance, the robot started fast 
and slowly deaccelerates (as seen with the blue line, 
but this is just flipped for visualization). But this made 
the robot jerk when starting to move so we planed to 
make the robot it slowly accelerate and then slowly 
decelerate (seen in the red line)
Page: 39

Name: Maxwell Date: 10/28/2025
Team Meeting Notes
Team Meeting - 10/28
Fixed robot torque problem
● We implemented the system onto our robot from our 10/22 
meeting, and put it in our drivetrain code (utilising the to_m 
and sm variables from the rotation-based drivetrain)
if to_m < sm/2:
        abs_tomove = abs((2*to_m) / sm)
    else:
        abs_tomove = abs((2*(to_m-sm)) / sm)
● Also added a torque control system for the driver, to switch 
between accuracy (low torque) for precision adjustments 
and high speed (high torque) for speeding across the field
Page: 40

Name: Maxwell Date: 10/28/2025
Team Meeting Notes
Team Meeting - 10/28
Updated robot torque solution
● Modified the demos graph from the last meeting to change 
with a ratio and instead of if to_m < sm/2 its now to_m < 
sm*0.6 as seen with the linear constant line in the middle.
○ Now the robot will still accelerate slowly, but will take 
longer to slow down for that accuracy
Page: 41

Name: Maxwell Date: 11/12/2025
Team Meeting Notes
Team Meeting - 11/12
Inertial sensor
● On 11/11, we had team meetings with leadership, got 
together, amongst other things
○ But most importantly, we received the inertial sensor, 
we will be using it now instead of the drivetrain system 
we have been using.
● And now, on the 12th, we have finally began our work on 
the inertial sensor
● The inertial sensor gives us only one variable for moving on 
axes, acceleration in G’s
○ But, this is not a problem since we know how to get 
time via our first drivetrain system, 
● With acceleration and time, we first thought that if we had a 
variable for setting our velocity and distance moved, if we 
add to those we can get how much we moved and make 
the robot stop when that variable is equal to how much we 
want to move
Cont. on next page
Page: 42

Name: Maxwell Date: 11/12/2025
Team Meeting Notes
Team Meeting - 11/12
Inertial sensor
● With acceleration and time, we first thought that if we had a 
variable for setting our velocity and distance moved, if we 
add to those we can get how much we moved and make 
the robot stop when that variable is equal to how much we 
want to move
○ We were not able to test this but we will be able to next

Page: 43

Name: Maxwell Date: 11/18/2025
Team Meeting Notes
Team Meeting - 11/18
Inertial sensor COMPLETE!
● We have done it! First try! The code from the previous day 
worked flawlessly, without error!
○ All we had to do now was make the motors change 
velocity similar to what we did on 10/22/25 & 10/28/25
Page: 44

Name: Maxwell Date: 11/18/2025
Team Meeting Notes
Team Meeting - 11/18
Inertial sensor rotation problem
● But, when doing turning, since the inertial sensor already 
had a variable for detecting the velocity of the turn in DPS 
(so we don't have to do acceleration to velocity) we just 
used the same system but for degrees instead of rotation.
○ But we ran into a problem, the velocity variable (velset, 
from the code snippet before), just was not being set to 
floor speed and going up and down and all that stuff 
(like in original drivetrain w/ the sensor)
● This was fixed with making velset equal
(velocity_curve(dist_moved,meters) * (max_speed - 
floor_speed)) + floor_speed
○ Why? Because velocity_curve first returns a 0 and with 
the one before, everything (including the +floor speed) 
was times it so it just equaled zero
○ Why did it work for moving forward? We think it was 
accounting for slight shifts on the floor so that gave it a 
kick start from zero
● We taught the new members how to code some more, and 
were able to get them to make their first drive code, which 
used the joysticks to move the robot

Name: Maxwell Date: 12/2/2025
Team Meeting Notes
Team Meeting - 12/2
Fixing drivetrains and robots
● Fixed our debugging robot, the wheels were loose
● Debugged our motor-based drivetrain and compressed 
the whole left right system to…
● We added the math.fabs in the to_move -= thing because 
when the robot moved backwards it went forever because 
the to move was going up instead of down (2 minuses are 
essentially a plus)
● And it turns out, when moving more than 24 inches, the 
inertial drivetrain code breaks down, most likely because 
of the calculations
○ Tried some solutions to fix the inertial move forward 
functions like using an actual kinematic equation, 
((veloc * elasped_time) + (0.5 * accel * elasped_time * 
elasped_time)), but that didn't help, the robot still 
moved too short of its goal

and somehow it worked!

an adjustable variable that is different for all robots

Page: 50

Name: Maxwell Date: 12/16/2025
Team Meeting Notes
Team Meeting - 12/16
Inertial sensor tweaks
● The multiplying the distance by 3 from inertial sensor code 
was in fact not a solution, and upon running it today 
removing it made the robot move normally
● BUT, when we got the competition robot we had to times it 
by 3 again to fix the movement issue (last time was on the 
test robot, much lighter and faster)
● We assume this is a motor issue, and build team will move 
the middle drive motors to the back so energy is more 
evenly distributed
● BUT, the turning still works perfectly, which is a massive 
improvement from the previous drivetrain system we had 
with the motor encoders, so we will NOT be going back!

Name: Novalee Date: 12/16/25
  Reflecting on Competition
 First competition of 2026
WENT WELL
We earned a Judge’s award!
We performed as a sturdy defense for our 
teammate’s
Output worked well and was reliable
We learned a lot about what we wanted to 
improve from our alliances.
Purpose: The document is made to reflect and improve from 
the mistakes and successes from last competition
NEEDS IMPROVEMENT
We decided we needed to cover our rubber band intake 
with mesh because the nature of the competition lead to 
the rubber bands getting tangled. 
We want to focus more on the autonomous portion, 
which means we need to give our code team lots of time 
with the ﬁnished robot.
Branching off the previous, we aim to give code team a 
full week to perfect the robot’s coding and autonomous. 
We need to tighten the gears surrounding our axles, 
because during the competition we noticed it would 
shake a worrisome amount. 
Our robot was much too slow, so we will try to make it 
more narrow and take some weight off of it. 
Next competition, our goal is to get the 
autonomous win point, stay within the top 10 
teams, and our ultimate goal is to win the 
excellence award.
